mappings.rationaleAI: Historiographical theory covering causation, periodization, and interpretation of primary sources (A.2, A.3), tracing historians from William of Malmesbury through Gibbon and Marx (A.1.a, A.1.c.iv). The Donation of Constantine forgery case illustrates source criticism methods (B.1). Philosophy of history and how knowledge of the past shapes identity (C).

Classical Greek historiography (A.1.a.ii) examining Herodotus's investigation of the Persian Wars, his interview-based research methods and reliance on diverse sources (B.1), and the background and vantage point of the author as traveler and observer (A.3).

Classical Greek historiography (A.1.a.ii) covering Thucydides' pioneering methodology as both participant and analyst of the Peloponnesian War. His identity as Athenian aristocrat and combatant exemplifies the background and vantage point of the author (A.3).

Islamic historiographical tradition (A.1.d.i) encompassing the unique importance Muslims attach to historical consciousness, Hadith literature as historical source, and the role of historical understanding in Islamic civilization.

Methodological approaches to historical writing about political ideas (A.2, A.3), including the Cambridge School of Skinner and Pocock. Explores how the study of past political thought informs contemporary dilemmas, bridging speculative philosophy of history (C).
A specialized type of historical writing examining emotions across time (A.2), connecting psychology, cultural history, and linguistics as auxiliary methods (A.3).

Global historical survey of childhood as a distinct type of historical writing (A.2), revealing how shifting definitions by class, religion, and geography illustrate the historian's vantage point (A.3).

Gender as an analytical framework represents a distinct type of historical writing (A.2). Challenges assumptions about historical subjecthood (A.3) and questions dominant narratives, engaging speculative philosophy of history (C).
Intellectual history from Enlightenment through postmodernism as a type of historical writing (A.2), exemplifying modern historiographical traditions (A.1.c.iv).
Cultural history as a distinct type of historical writing (A.2), examining literature, music, art, film, and media as expressions of collective values.
Comparative economic history as a type of historical writing (A.2) analyzing why some countries prospered while others remained poor.
Archaeology as an auxiliary discipline (B.2.b), relying on material remains as sources (B.1) and using radiometric dating techniques (B.2.o).
Archaeological investigation of biblical material evidence (B.2.b), covering excavation sites and inscriptions as sources (B.1), with epigraphy (B.2.g).
Anthropology as an auxiliary discipline for interpreting historical sources (B.2.a).

mappings.vsiAuthor: John A. Matthews, David T. Herbert
mappings.rationaleAI: Geography as an auxiliary discipline for historical research (B.2.i).
Linguistics as an auxiliary discipline (B.2.l), covering language evolution and change over time.
Egyptian hieroglyphs as a subject of epigraphy (B.2.g) and paleography (B.2.m), covering the Rosetta Stone decipherment.
Writing systems as written materials for historical research (B.1), with decipherment connecting to epigraphy (B.2.g) and paleography (B.2.m).
Study of ancient Greek and Roman civilizations encompassing classical historiography (A.1.a.ii), with textual culture connecting to textual criticism (B.2.q).
Byzantine history directly relevant to Byzantine historiography (A.1.b.ii).
Intellectual history of the Enlightenment directly relevant to Enlightenment historiography (A.1.c.iii).
Prehistory relying on material evidence (B.1), with archaeology as the primary auxiliary discipline (B.2.b).
Radiometric dating as an auxiliary discipline for historical sources (B.2.o).
History of time measurement directly relevant to chronology (B.2.d).
Philosophy addressing fundamental questions connects to philosophy of history (C).
Rousseau's critique of civilization connects to speculative philosophy of history (C.2).
Biography as scholarly practice represents a distinct type of historical writing (A.2), with methodological questions illustrating factors in writing (A.3).
Autobiography as life writing represents a type of historical writing (A.2), with memoirs and diaries as written sources (B.1).
Oral history as methodology (A.2), with spoken testimony as a source alongside written materials (B.1).
Dictionaries as written materials reflecting social assumptions, serving as historical sources (B.1).
Forensic science methods provide material remains as sources for investigation (B.1).
Psychoanalysis as an auxiliary discipline for interpreting historical sources (B.2.n).
Near Eastern historiography covering Mesopotamian civilizations (A.1.a.i), with archaeological evidence as sources (B.1).
Ancient Egyptian civilization as Near Eastern historiography (A.1.a.i), with archaeology (B.2.b) and material sources (B.1).
Geomorphology connects to geography (B.2.i), with radiometric dating methods (B.2.o).
